reasoning: The disagreement is not a substantive security disagreement. The deepseek model completed a full audit (PKGBUILD + 10 source files) and found no malicious behavior. The qwen model also reviewed PKGBUILD and 10 files (all SAFE) but its run terminated with an infrastructure error ('[Errno 122] Disk quota exceeded'), which produced an INCONCLUSIVE status purely due to a system failure, not a security finding. Both models independently judged the PKGBUILD and overlapping source files as safe. The PKGBUILD fetches sources from the official rustfs GitHub org with SHA256 checksums, has no pkgver()/network ops in global scope, and installs only a single binary with no .install hooks or systemd units. The reviewed TypeScript files are standard frontend code (API client, AWS SigV4 signing, policy parsers). Verdict is safe with medium confidence because not all build-relevant files were exhaustively reviewed (notably the Rust source and the build commands' downloaded npm lifecycle scripts).
disagreement_analysis: The 'inconclusive' verdict from qwen was caused by an infrastructure failure (disk quota exceeded) mid-run, not a security concern. Every file qwen actually assessed (including PKGBUILD and config.ts/eslint.config.js) was marked SAFE. This is a false disagreement driven by tooling error, not by conflicting analysis.
coverage_issues: Both models focused exclusively on console-0.1.8 TypeScript files; neither reviewed the main rustfs Rust source tree, which is the actual packaged binary.
The console-0.1.8.tar.gz binary tarball was skipped by both (expected for binary files, but the build extracts/uses it).

Neither model deeply scrutinized the npm lifecycle script risk introduced by pnpm '--dangerously-allow-all-builds' during build() — only deepseek flagged it as a hygienic concern without examining package.json scripts or the pnpm lockfile.
qwen's audit did not complete due to disk quota error, so its coverage is partial/aborted.
false_positives: qwen's INCONCLUSIVE verdict is effectively a false positive for a security issue — it stems from an infrastructure error, not actual suspicious content.
deepseek's noting of '--dangerously-allow-all-builds' is correctly contextualized as a hygienic supply-chain note, not an active malicious indicator — appropriately not escalated.
false_negatives: pnpm '--dangerously-allow-all-builds' (and '--pm-on-fail=ignore') runs arbitrary npm package lifecycle scripts at build time — this is the highest-value build-time execution surface and was not investigated by reviewing package.json/dependency scripts.
The Rust source code that compiles into the installed binary was never reviewed by either model; a malicious build.rs or vendored dependency would be missed.
re_audit_focus: build() function npm/pnpm lifecycle execution: examine console package.json scripts, postinstall hooks, and pnpm-lock.yaml for the dependencies pulled by --dangerously-allow-all-builds
Rust build.rs files and Cargo.lock in the rustfs source tarball
Re-run qwen (or a stronger model) in an environment with sufficient disk to complete the aborted audit
learnings: Distinguish infrastructure failures (disk quota, network timeout) from genuine inconclusive security verdicts — these should be retried automatically rather than surfaced as disagreement-triggering 'inconclusive' results.

When a PKGBUILD invokes package-manager flags that enable arbitrary build scripts (--dangerously-allow-all-builds, pnpm/npm postinstall), the file selector should prioritize package.json, lockfiles, and any referenced scripts as build-relevant rather than treating them as dev tooling.

File selection over-indexed on frontend TypeScript (.ts) library files that are not executed during makepkg; for a Rust package, build.rs and Cargo manifests are higher-priority build-time execution surfaces.
Add disk space/quota preflight checks before bulk audits to avoid mid-run aborts that pollute disagreement analysis.
Both models gave consistent SAFE verdicts on overlapping files, indicating the disagreement-resolution trigger should weight completed vs aborted runs differently.
